questions.question: Describe the algorithm to insert a new node into a binary search tree (BST). Include the steps and provide the recursive pseudo-code.
keyPoints: Compare key with root; if less, go left; if greater, go right
Recur until null child found, then insert new node
Time complexity O(h) where h is height of tree
Pseudo-code should handle both empty tree and non‑empty tree cases

questions.question: Explain the procedure to delete a node from a BST, covering the three possible cases: leaf node, node with one child, and node with two children.
keyPoints: One child: replace node with its child
Two children: find inorder successor (or predecessor), copy its value, delete successor
Maintain BST property after deletion; handle re‑linking of subtrees

questions.question: Given the sequence of keys: 30, 15, 45, 10, 25, 35, 50, 5, 12, 20, 27. Insert them one by one into an initially empty BST. Draw the resulting tree. Then delete the key 15 from the BST, showing the tree after each step of the deletion algorithm. Provide the final BST.
keyPoints: Insertion follows BST property; show tree after each insertion
Deletion of 15 (node with two children) requires finding inorder successor (20) or predecessor, copy, then delete leaf
Show intermediate trees after each deletion step
Final BST must satisfy ordering property; height and balance may change

questions.question: Write a recursive algorithm (pseudo‑code or C++/Java code) to search for a key in a BST. Analyse its time and space complexity. Using the BST constructed in Question 3, trace the algorithm to search for the key 35 and also for a key that does not exist, e.g., 40. Show the recursion trace.
keyPoints: Pseudo‑code: if root == null return false; if key == root.key return true; if key < root.key return search(root.left, key) else search(root.right, key)
Time complexity O(h) average O(log n) worst O(n); space O(h) due to recursion stack
